1: The altar of burnt offering of acacia wood, five cubits square and three cubits high. The altar of sacrifice: the first thing the approaching worshiper encounters at the court entrance. The cross is the first thing one encounters in approaching God through Christ: “we have been justified by his blood” (Romans 5:9). No access to the Father except through the altar of the Son's sacrifice (John 14:6).
2: The horns on each of its four corners, one piece with it, overlaid with bronze. The horns of the altar at each corner: sacrificial power extending in all four directions, toward every people. The atonement is universal in scope: “he is the atoning sacrifice for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world” (1 John 2:2). The horns also provide asylum (1 Kings 1:50): the altar of sacrifice is also the place of refuge (Hebrews 6:18).
3: Pots, shovels, basins, forks, and firepans — all of bronze. Every instrument of sacrifice is bronze (judgment material). The cross had its specific instruments (nails, spear, vinegar, crown of thorns) — each an instrument in the judgment-death Christ bore for his people. The full apparatus of the sacrifice belongs to the judgment-theme.
4: A bronze grating under the altar's ledge, extending halfway down. The structural detail ensuring the altar's function. The cross's specific structure was the Father's deliberate design (Acts 2:23: “handed over to you by God's deliberate plan and foreknowledge”). “It pleased YHWH to crush him” (Isaiah 53:10) — the sacrifice was not accidental but architecturally purposed.
5: Rings on the four corners of the grating for the carrying poles. The bronze altar, like the ark and table, has carrying rings: the sacrifice is portable. The gospel carries Christ's sacrifice into every place: “we preach Christ crucified” (1 Corinthians 1:23). The cross is not fixed at Golgotha in its redemptive reach but “proclaimed in all creation under heaven” (Colossians 1:23).
6: Poles of acacia wood overlaid with bronze. Bronze-covered acacia for the sacrifice altar's poles: judgment-quality dominates here, not gold. The gospel message bears the judgment-bronze, not prestige-gold. The cross is an offense (1 Corinthians 1:23) precisely because it is the judgment-event, not a glory-display.
7: The poles inserted into the rings — the altar was hollow, made of boards. The hollow altar: the sacrifice happens within the frame. The judgment of God burned at the center of Christ's passion (Mark 15:34: “My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”). The hollow structure allows the fire to burn inside — as the fire of divine wrath burned within Christ for our sin.
8: The bronze basin and its stand made from the mirrors of the women who served at the entrance to the tent. The laver made from bronze mirrors: instruments for seeing oneself transformed into instruments for cleansing others. The women who gave their mirrors gave what was most personal and self-focused. Christ gives what is most personal — his own body and blood — to become the cleansing of others (John 13:5-10; Ephesians 5:26: “cleansing her by the washing of water with the word”).
9: The court: the south side had hangings of fine twisted linen, one hundred cubits long, with twenty pillars in twenty bronze sockets. The court defined the boundary of the sacred space. The church as a defined community set apart for God's purposes (2 Corinthians 6:17). Bronze sockets: the court stands on judgment — awareness of YHWH's holiness governs the perimeter of sacred space.
10: Twenty pillars in twenty bronze sockets, with silver hooks and bands on the pillars. Bronze sockets (judgment foundation) with silver joinings (redemption): the court's pillars stand in judgment-metal but are held together by ransom-silver. The church lives in the context of God's holiness and judgment while being held together by the redemption Christ accomplished (Ephesians 1:7).
11: The north side: one hundred cubits, twenty pillars in twenty bronze sockets, silver hooks and bands. Uniform boundary on every side: the same holiness, the same judgment-foundation, the same redemption-joinings surround the sacred space entirely. One foundation, one boundary, one entrance.
12: The west side: fifty cubits, ten pillars in ten sockets. Even the outer boundary takes its proportions from the inner sanctuary. Everything in the church's visible life is shaped by its innermost devotion to Christ.
13: The east side, toward the sunrise, fifty cubits. The gate through which one enters is on the east: the approach to God through Christ is from the direction of the rising sun. Christ is described with solar imagery: “the sun of righteousness will rise with healing in its wings” (Malachi 4:2). He is the light who comes from the east (Matthew 2:2).
14: Hangings of fifteen cubits on one side of the gate, with three pillars in three sockets. The gate area: defined passage flanking the entrance. The approach to Christ is a visible journey through a defined passage: “enter through the narrow gate” (Matthew 7:13-14). The gate is specific, visible, and bounded.
15: The same for the other side: fifteen cubits, three pillars in three sockets. The symmetrical flanking: the approach to Christ is the same from every angle. “There is no difference between Jew and Gentile — the same Lord is Lord of all and richly blesses all who call on him” (Romans 10:12).
16: All the hangings around the court were of fine twisted linen. The perimeter defined entirely by fine linen: the outer boundary of the sacred space is made of the same material as the inner tabernacle curtains. The visible church reflects the same righteousness that characterizes the innermost holy place. Revelation 19:8: “Fine linen, bright and clean, was given her to wear.”
17: Bronze sockets for the pillars, silver hooks and bands, silver overlaying the tops, all the pillars banded with silver. The court pillars: bronze base (judgment), silver body and top (redemption). The entire visible structure stands in judgment but is clothed in redemption-silver. The visible church is the community of those under judgment but clothed in the ransom Christ paid.
18: The screen for the gate of the court, embroidered in blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and fine twisted linen, twenty cubits wide and five cubits high. The entrance screen uses the same blue-purple-scarlet-linen design as the tabernacle entrance (Exodus 36:37) and the inner veil (Exodus 36:35). The same Christ who is the veil (Hebrews 10:20) is also the gate (John 10:9), described in the same colors at every level of approach.
19: Four pillars and four sockets of bronze, hooks of silver, tops and bands of silver. The gate screen's four pillars: bronze sockets, silver adornment. The entrance stands in judgment (bronze) but is decorated with redemption (silver). The approach to Christ begins by reckoning with judgment and ends by receiving ransom: “while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Romans 5:8).
20: All the pegs for the tabernacle and the court all around were of bronze. The pegs anchoring the entire structure to the ground: bronze throughout. The church is anchored in the judgment-reality of Christ's cross: “we have this hope as an anchor for the soul, firm and secure” (Hebrews 6:19). The bronze pegs are the historical, once-for-all character of the atonement that anchors everything.
21: These are the accounts of the tabernacle, recorded at the commandment of Moses. The complete accounting of the entire construction: total materials, total work, every specification. The completeness of the record corresponds to the completeness of Christ's work: “It is finished” (John 19:30). Nothing is left out, nothing is missing, no specification is unmet.
22: Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that YHWH commanded Moses. Bezalel (“in the shadow of God”) from the tribe of Judah: the royal lineage of the Messiah (Genesis 49:10; Hebrews 7:14). The master craftsman of the tabernacle comes from the tribe through which the one who is the temple will come. Christ, from the tribe of Judah, is both the craftsman of the new temple (Hebrews 3:3-4) and the temple itself (John 2:21).
23: With him was Oholiab of the tribe of Dan — an engraver, designer, and embroiderer in blue, purple, scarlet, and fine linen. Two craftsmen: Bezalel (Judah, south/royal) and Oholiab (Dan, north/artisan). The two-tribe collaboration type of the church gathered from every origin: the new temple is constructed by those YHWH has gifted from every background (1 Corinthians 12:4-7: “there are different kinds of gifts, but the same Spirit”).
24: All the gold used for the work — the gold from the wave offering — was twenty-nine talents and 730 shekels. Approximately one ton of gold lavished on the dwelling of God. The new covenant's dwelling is built with the gold of Christ's own divine nature — but at greater cost: “not with perishable things like silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ” (1 Peter 1:18-19).
25: The silver from those of the congregation who were numbered was a hundred talents and 1,775 shekels. Every shekel comes from the ransom-payment of every counted Israelite (Exodus 30:11-16: a half-shekel per man as ransom for his life). The tabernacle's silver is collected ransom money. The church stands on the ransom Christ paid: “you were bought at a price” (1 Corinthians 6:20).
26: A beka a head — half a shekel — for each of the 603,550 men counted. The ransom price is equal for all: half a shekel per man, regardless of wealth or status. The atonement is equal: “there is no difference, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and all are justified freely by his grace” (Romans 3:22-24). The cross gives the same atonement to all.
27: The hundred talents of silver were used to cast the sockets for the sanctuary and for the veil — one talent per socket, one hundred sockets from the hundred talents. One talent of ransom silver per socket: each foundation socket is the ransom price of the people. The whole structural standing of the tabernacle is the ransom. The church's entire existence rests on the one sufficient ransom paid by Christ: one foundation (1 Corinthians 3:11), one ransom payment (Matthew 20:28).
28: The remaining 1,775 shekels used to make hooks for the pillars, to overlay their tops, and to make bands. The ransom silver not only founds the tabernacle but joins and crowns its visible boundary. The redemption Christ accomplished not only founds the church but holds it together and beautifies it: “Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her... to present her to himself as a radiant church” (Ephesians 5:25-27).
29: The bronze of the wave offering was seventy talents and 2,400 shekels. Approximately two tons of judgment-metal in the tabernacle: altar, laver, court sockets, pegs — all bronze. The cross is the supreme judgment-event: “God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement” (Romans 3:25) — the full weight of divine judgment absorbed by the one sacrifice.
30: With it he made the sockets for the entrance, the bronze altar and its grating, all the altar's utensils, the court sockets, and the gate sockets. Every threshold, every surface of sacrifice, every point of encounter with YHWH's holiness stands on judgment-bronze. There is no access to the holy God that does not pass through the judgment Christ bore on the cross (John 14:6; Hebrews 9:22).
31: The pegs for the court all around, and all the pegs for the tabernacle surrounding it. The final accounting: every anchor point is bronze. The entire structure is anchored to the earth by judgment-metal. The church is anchored in history by the specific, once-for-all judgment-event of the cross: “Jesus Christ and him crucified” (1 Corinthians 2:2) is the anchor of the whole structure. Nothing holds except what is anchored to the cross.
